coredata.dc:title: The fabrication of highly ordered fluorescent molecularly imprinted mesoporous microspheres for the selective sensing of sparfloxacin in biological samples
coredata.dc:creator: Geng, Yuanyuan
Guo, Manli
Tan, Jiean
Huang, Shuyi
Tang, Youwen
Tan, Lei
Liang, Yong
coredata.dc:description: This paper describes an alternative strategy for the preparation of highly ordered molecularly imprinted mesoporous silica microspheres (MIMSM) for the selective fluorescence sensing of sparfloxacin in biological samples. We designed and synthesized a new functional monomer through the thiol-ene click chemistry reaction of 3-(methacryloyloxy) propyltrimethoxysilane and 3-mercaptopropionic acid. Sparfloxacin was selected as a template, and Mn-doped ZnS quantum dots served as the signal transducing element. This one-pot strategy was used to fabricate the MIMSM. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy demonstrated successful embedding of a functional monomer in the mesoporous silica microspheres, and the highly ordered, periodic hexagonal mesoporous structure of MIMSM was confirmed with small angle X-ray diffraction, transmission electron microscopy, and N2 adsorption-desorption measurements. The fluorescence intensity of the MIMSM had linear correlation with 0.05–2.0 μg/mL sparfloxacin. There was a rapid response time and excellent selectivity for structural analogues. Under optimal conditions, the limit of detection was 0.012 μg/mL for sparfloxacin. This method was then used with serum samples to achieve recoveries of 88.8–102%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molecularly imprinted polymer
Mesoporous
Quantum dots
Sensor
Click reaction
Sparfloxacin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of L-cysteine modified Mn-doped ZnS quantum dots
Time response of the MIMSM for fluorescence detection of sparfloxacin
Effect of pH on fluorescence detection of sparfloxacin
Using MIMSM for selective detection of sparfloxacin in real human serum

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the preparation of highly ordered fluorescent molecularly imprinted mesoporous silica microspheres.

FT-IR spectra of (A) MCM-41, MIMSM and NIMSM, (B) MIMSM before and after elution, (C) small angle X-ray diffraction patterns of MIMSM, (D) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of MIMSM (inset: pore size distribution calculated from the adsorption branch).
(A, B) SEM image of MIMSM, (C, D) TEM image of MIMSM.
Equilibrium adsorption kinetics of MIMSM and NIMSM to sparfloxacin (0.5 μg/mL).
The effect of pH on detection of sparfloxacin (0.5 μg/mL) by NIMSM and MIMSM.

Fluorescence spectrum of (A) MIMSM; (B) NIMSM with different concentrations of sparfloxacin range from 0.05 μg/mL-2.0 μg/mL, (C) and (D) are the sparfloxacin concentration calibration curves respectively.
Selectivity of MIMSM and NIMSM toward sparfloxacin and its structural analogs; bottom panels are the chemical structures.
Recovery for the determination of sparfloxacin in human serum samples.
The summary of different methods for the detection of sparfloxacin in biological samples.

head.ce:title: The fabrication of highly ordered fluorescent molecularly imprinted mesoporous microspheres for the selective sensing of sparfloxacin in biological samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Environment, South China Normal University, 510006, Guangzhou, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangzhou Center for Disease Control and Prevention, 510440, Guangzhou, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangzhou Center for Disease Control and Prevention

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper describes an alternative strategy for the preparation of highly ordered molecularly imprinted mesoporous silica microspheres (MIMSM) for the selective fluorescence sensing of sparfloxacin in biological samples. We designed and synthesized a new functional monomer through the thiol-ene click chemistry reaction of 3-(methacryloyloxy) propyltrimethoxysilane and 3-mercaptopropionic acid. Sparfloxacin was selected as a template, and Mn-doped ZnS quantum dots served as the signal transducing element. This one-pot strategy was used to fabricate the MIMSM. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy demonstrated successful embedding of a functional monomer in the mesoporous silica microspheres, and the highly ordered, periodic hexagonal mesoporous structure of MIMSM was confirmed with small angle X-ray diffraction, transmission electron microscopy, and N2 adsorption-desorption measurements. The fluorescence intensity of the MIMSM had linear correlation with 0.05–2.0 μg/mL sparfloxacin. There was a rapid response time and excellent selectivity for structural analogues. Under optimal conditions, the limit of detection was 0.012 μg/mL for sparfloxacin. This method was then used with serum samples to achieve recoveries of 88.8–102%.

ce:section.ce:para: Molecular imprinting creates tailor-made binding sites using the shape, size, and functional groups of the template molecules in tandem with a polymer matrix [1,2]. Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) have attracted extensive attention from the scientific and industrial communities and are widely used in sample pretreatment [3,4], chromatographic separation [5,6], sensing [7–9], catalysts [10], and drug delivery [11]. Although artificial synthesis gives the MIPs remarkable robustness, highly physical stability, low cost, and cyclic utilization, traditional MIPs often have low binding capacities and low mass transfer speeds [12,13].

Mesoporous silica materials have high surface area, ordered structure, uniform tunable pore size distribution, and excellent chemical stability—this makes them an ideal extraction material [14,15]. As an imprinting matrix material, mesoporous silica carries recognition sites near the surface due to its large pore volume and nano-sized pore wall thickness [16,17]. This gives the template molecules high site accessibility, and it accelerates the speed of adsorption and separation [18,19]. The rigid and highly cross-linked structure of the xerogel silica allows the creation of delicate imprint sites with a high degree of shape selectivity versus more flexible organic polymers. Creating molecularly imprinted mesoporous silica remains challenging because the number of silica precursor functional monomers available for molecular imprinting is limited [20]. However, it is also important to select suitable functional monomers that can strongly interact with the template and form specific complexes prior to polymerization [21].

Quantum dots (QDs) are a relatively new fluorescent material. Versus traditional organic dyes, QDs have brighter photoluminescence, better photostability, narrower emission, broader excitation, a larger Stokes shift, lower background noise, and better biocompatibility [22,23]. Thus, they are widely used in chemo/bio-sensing and bio-imaging [24,25]. Combining the specific recognition properties of MIPs and the excellent fluorescence properties of QDs for sensing systems is particularly intriguing [26–29]. However, the organic polymers matrix coated QDs have some limitations such as poor site accessibility, and the highly cross-linked matrix is likely to hinder template molecules from moving freely, which limits its analytical applications [30,31].

Sparfloxacin (SPFX) is the third-generation quinolone antibiotic. It is widely used in veterinary and human medicine to treat respiratory, urinary, and digestive infections as well as skin and soft tissue [32,33]. Current methods to detect sparfloxacin include high performance liquid chromatography coupled with UV-detection, high performance liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry, capillary electrophoresis, etc [34]. However, most of these methods require complex and time-consuming sample pretreatment procedures and expensive analytical instrumentations [35]. Here, we demonstrate the feasibility of an alternative strategy for producing fluorescent molecularly imprinted ordered mesoporous microspheres for high specific and selective sensing of sparfloxacin. Thiol-ene click chemistry was used to synthesize a new functional monomer. Sparfloxacin was selected as the template, and Mn-doped ZnS quantum dots served as the signal transducing element. This was integrated with a one-pot strategy to fabricate the MIMSM. The resulting MIMSM showed a highly ordered and periodic hexagonal mesoporous structure. Under optimal conditions, good linear correlation was achieved for sparfloxacin from 0.05 to 2.0 μg/mL with a rapid response time and excellent selectivity for structural analogues. The fluorescence sensing of sparfloxacin using these MIMSMs offers a new and general strategy for the sensitive and selective detection of the target antibiotic in biological samples.

All chemicals were of analytical grade. Tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) was purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). The 3-mercaptopropionic acid (3-MAP), L-cysteine, γ-(methacryloyloxy) propyltrimethoxysilane (KH-570) and aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTES) were obtained from Macklin (Shanghai, China). MnCl2.4H2O, Na2S.9H2O, ZnSO4.7H2O, NaOH, isopropanol, triethylamine and dimethylformamide were from Tianjian Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) was obtained from Guangdong Qiyun Biotech. Co., Ltd. (Guangdong, China). Sparfloxacin, norfloxacin, ciprofloxacin, gatifloxacin and clindamycin were purchased from Saanen Science and Technology Company (Shanghai, China). Other chemicals were purchased from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Company and used without further purification. All solutions were prepared with double deionized water (DDW).

The morphology and microstructure of MIMSM was studied using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) on a JEM-2100HR at 120 kV (JEOL, Japan) and scanning electron microscope (SEM) on a Hitachi S-4800 FESEM (Tokyo, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra (4000–400 cm−1) in KBr were recorded on a Nicolet 6700 FT-IR Spectrometer (Foster City, CA, USA). The fluorescence spectra were collected from an F-4600 Hitachi spectrometer (Tokyo, Japan). The N2 adsorption-desorption measurements used a surface area and pore size analyzer. Surface areas were calculated according to the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method, and the pore volume and pore size distribution were calculated using the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) model. The small angle X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were obtained from a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). The thermogravimetric analysis used a synchronous thermal analyzer NETZSCH STA 409 PC (NETZSCH, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of L-cysteine modified Mn-doped ZnS quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of L-cysteine-modified Mn-doped ZnS quantum dots used here was done following a literature method with some modification [36]. Briefly, 0.1442 g ZnSO4.7H2O, 0.0047 g MnCl2.4H2O and 0.1210 g L-cysteine were added to 25 mL of ultrapure water with slow stirring to dissolve completely, resulting in a 5 wt% Mn doping. The freshly prepared 1 M sodium hydroxide solution was added to the above mixture dropwise to adjust its pH to 11.0. After purging with nitrogen for half an hour to remove dissolved oxygen, 0.2402 g of Na2S.9H2O dissolved in 5 mL of ultrapure water was added into above oxygen-free solution drop wise with a constant pressure dropping funnel. The mixture was stirred under reflux for 14 h at 50 °C in open air. The quantum dot solution was then centrifuged after precipitation with isopropanol. After several washings, the purified Mn-ZnS QDs solution was stored in the dark at 4 °C for further use. Solid samples were obtained after vacuum drying at room temperature.

The procedure for synthetically producing the functional monomer used in this work is similar to the method previously described in literature [37,38]. In a 10 mL round bottom flask, 87.14 μL of 3-mercaptopropionic acid (3-MAP) and 248.35 μL γ-(methacryloyloxy) propyltrimethoxysilane (KH-570) was dissolved in 5 mL absolute ethanol, and the pH of the solution was adjusted to 8.0 with triethylamine. The reaction was stirred at 40 °C for 2 h in a water bath. The product was stored at 4 °C until further use. The resultant product was characterized by high-solution mass spectrometry (Fig. S1), 1H NMR (Fig. S2), 13C NMR (Fig. S3), and FT-IR (Fig. S4). 1H NMR (400 MHz, CDCl3, 25℃, TMS): δ = 4.08 ppm (d, J = 6.40 Hz,2 H; Hd), δ = 3.58 ppm (s, 9 H; Ha), δ = 2.77 ppm (d, J = 7.20 Hz, 2 H; Hh), δ = 2.70 ppm (t, J = 6.40 Hz, 2 H; Hi), δ = 2.68 ppm (m, 1 H; Hg), δ = 2.61 ppm (m, 2 H; Hj), δ = 1.69 ppm (m, 2 H; Hc), δ = 1.25 ppm (d, J = 7.20 Hz, 3 H; Hf), δ = 0.69 ppm (t, J = 8.40 Hz, 2 H; Hb). 13C NMR (400 MHz, CDCl3, 25℃, TMS): δ = 176.47 ppm (Ce), δ = 175.17 ppm (Ck), δ = 66.60 ppm (Cd), δ = 50.54 ppm (Ca), δ = 40.21 ppm (Cg), δ = 38.21 ppm (Ch), δ = 34.56 ppm (Cj), δ = 27.16 ppm (Ci), δ = 21.84 ppm (Cf), δ = 19.40 ppm (Cc), δ = 16.82 ppm (Cb).

Typically, 13 mg of sparfloxacin was dissolved in 2 mL dimethylformamide with 40 μL APTES; 0.16 mmol of the functional monomer was added subsequently. The mixture was magnetically stirred at room temperature for 2 h to form a pre-action complex. Next, 0.2 g CTAB and 0.7 mL NaOH solution (2.0 M) were dissolved in 100 mL ultrapure water. When the mixture was heated to 80 °C, 1 mL TEOS, 3 mL of an L-cysteine-modified Mn-doped ZnS quantum dot solution and above pre-action complex were sequentially added with a constant pressure dropping funnel. The resulting mixture was stirred at 80 °C for 6 h. After the reaction was completed and cooled to room temperature, the supernatant was removed by centrifugation at 12,000 rpm/min for 10 min.

The precipitate was repeatedly washed with 80% ethanol/water (v/v) to remove sparfloxacin and CTAB. The product was placed in a vacuum oven and dried at 40 °C to obtain the solid MIMSM. The non-imprinted mesoporous silica microspheres (NIMSM) were similarly synthesized as described above except that no sparfloxacin (template) was added.

The serum samples were provided by healthy volunteers and stored at −20 °C. Prior to use, the serum samples were placed in a 37 °C water bath with slow shaking until dissolved. Spike recovery experiments were performed because no sparfloxacin was detected in the serum samples. The measurement procedure is based on the previous report with slight modification [39]. After the serum samples were diluted 20-fold with double deionized water, a series of different concentrations of sparfloxacin solution and 1 mg/mL MIMSM solutions were added. The mixture was allowed to stand for 30 min and then centrifuged at 5000 rpm/min for 10 min. The supernatant was removed, and the MIMSM were re-suspended in an equal volume of pH 6.0 phosphate buffer for fluorescence measurements.

The fluorescence spectrophotometer measurements used slit widths of 10 nm for excitation and emission. The photomultiplier tube voltage was 400 V, and the excitation wavelength was 302 nm. Before each measurement, a series of standard solutions of sparfloxacin for calibration was prepared by dissolving an appropriate amount of sparfloxacin in pH 6.0 PBS and stored at 4 °C. Then, 1 mg/mL MIMSM or NIMSM solution, 3 mL PBS, and different concentrations of sparfloxacin (1.0 mL) were sequentially added to a 5 mL calibrated test tube. Each sample was measured three times in parallel and averaged.

Silica is a highly cross-linked rigid matrix that suitable for the formation of delicate recognition sites. However, slow diffusion through bulk silica can severely hamper access to imprint sites, and it can cause a problem for molecular imprinting [40]. The choice of channels instead of closed pores increases the diffusion of the template molecule to reach an imprint site and allowing for easier flow through the material. This type of porosity can be created using a sol-gel approach involving a micelle template in aqueous media, to generate a mesoporous material [41]. In general, the molecularly imprinted mesoporous silica can be divided into two categories according to the synthetic methods. The first one is surface imprints grafting on mesoporous silica and the other one is synthesizing mesoporous silica with surface imprints [42]. By cocondensation the template-monomers system with siloxane species allowing the development of imprinting cavities on the pore surface of the mesoporous silica. As shown in Fig. 1 , we designed to create imprint sites by exploiting template-functional monomer interactions to create imprinting cavities in the mesoporous silica. Fig. 1 shows that a new silanized functional monomer was synthesized with 3-(methacryloyloxy)propyltrimethoxysilane (KH-570) and 3-mercaptopropionic acid (3-MAP) via thiol-ene click reaction. The click chemistry is highly efficient under mild conditions and environmentally friendly [43,44]. This can help directly synthesize monomers without any purification steps. Next, a sol-gel method was used for the one-pot preparation of the MIMSM. Quantum dots were used as antennae for signal amplification and optical output, sparfloxacin was the template, and APTES served as a functional monomer.

The FT-IR spectra of MCM-41, MIMSM, and NIMSM were performed to study their chemical composition. Fig. 2 (A) shows that the absorption peaks at 1085 cm−1, 951 cm−1 and 800 cm−1 had characteristic peaks of Si-O-Si, Si-O-H, and Si-O, respectively. This indicated that MCM-41, NIMSM and MIMSM were mostly silica. The NIMSM and MIMSM had a new absorption peak at 1700 cm−1 that was not seen in MCM-41. This represents the stretching vibration of CO and preliminarily confirmed that the functional monomer was successfully integrated into the skeletons of the mesoporous materials. Fig. 2(B) shows the FT-IR spectra of MIMSM before and after elution. The absorption peaks at 2924 cm−1 and 2854 cm−1 before elution were ascribed to the stretching vibration of C–H. They almost completely disappeared after elution indicating that CTAB was removed completely [45].

Fig. 2(C) shows the small angle XRD patterns of MIMSM. Three different diffraction peaks at 2θ = 2.3°, 3.8° and 4.4° were observed corresponding to the (100), (110), and (200) reflections of MCM-41, respectively. In addition, the three relevant d-spacing values were 38.4, 23.2 and 20.1 Å for a hexagonal lattice parameter of a = 44.3 Å [41].

Nitrogen adsorption-desorption experiments were performed to further confirm the mesoporous structure of MIMSM. Fig. 2(D) shows the typical type IV isotherm curves with an obvious adsorption hysteresis loop. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area obtained from the nitrogen isotherms was 1115.20 m2/g, and the pore volume was 1.13 cm3/g. The pore size distribution of MIMSM was 2.9–3.4 nm, and the average pore diameter was 3.1 nm as calculated from the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method. These results indicate that MIMSM had a well-defined mesoporous structure.

The High-resolution transmission electron microscopy image reveals that the Mn-doped QDs are spherical in shape and almost uniform in size, with a diameter of approximately 4 nm (Fig. S5). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) studies report the size and morphology of the MIMSM. Fig. 3 (A) and (B) showed that the MIMSM were spherical and nearly uniform in size (∼100 nm diameter). Fig. 3(C) and (D) show that the MIMSM exhibited a well-defined channel structure and a highly ordered cubic lattice arrangement demonstrating a typical periodic MCM-41 mesoporous structure that was consistent with the XRD data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Time response of the MIMSM for fluorescence detection of sparfloxacin
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 shows the time response of MIMSM in detecting sparfloxacin. The fluorescence intensity gradually weakened after sparfloxacin was added to the MIMSM solution. The intensity became stable after about 5 min. The same fluorescence quenching phenomena was seen in the MIMSM, but it took 15 min to reach equilibrium with adsorption and desorption at much lower fluorescence intensity. These results indicate that the equilibrium adsorption of NIMSM was faster than MIMSM. This is because sparfloxacin took more time to penetrate into the specific imprinted caves. Therefore, we set the optimal response time to 15 min for subsequent experiments. Interestingly, the mesoporous structured fluorescence MIPs with 15 min of equilibrium adsorption time had a faster response than common MIPs with non-mesoporous structure whose equilibrium adsorption time usually require about 30 min. This was mainly attributed to the mesoporous structure and improved accessibility of recognition sites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on fluorescence detection of sparfloxacin
ce:section.ce:para: Next, the effect of pH on the performance of the MIMSM and NIMSM was investigated. Fig. 5 shows that when the pH of the solution increased from 5.0 to 8.0, the imprinting effect first increased and then decreased before reaching a maximum at pH 6.0. One possible explanation was that the amino group of sparfloxacin was susceptible to protonation at pH 5.0–6.0, and this weakens the binding ability of the template toward the imprinted cavities. When the pH increased from 6.0 to 8.0, the carboxyl group of the functional monomer KH-3-MAP was easily deprotonated resulting in a decrease in the binding capacity. In addition, non-specific adsorption could be weakened in slightly acidic solutions. Therefore, we selected pH 6.0 as the optimal pH value of phosphate buffer solution for subsequent experiments.

MIMSM and NIMSM showed fluorescence quenching when the from 0.05 to 2.0 μg/mL sparfloxacin (Fig. 6 (A) and (B)). MIMSM showed larger fluorescence quenching efficiency than NIMSM due to the specific recognition of imprinted cavities on the sparfloxacin template. The fluorescence quenching behavior in this system followed the Stern-Volmer equation:
F0/F = 1+KSV [Cq]
where F and F0 are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of the quencher, respectively; Cq is the concentration of the quencher; and KSV is the Stern-Volmer constant [46].

As shown in Fig. 6(C) and (D), both the calibration plots of (F0/F-1) versus Cq of MIMSM and NIMSM showed a good linear relationship for sparfloxacin from 0.05 to 2.0 μg/mL. The correlation coefficients were both 0.998. The limit of detection was calculated following the 3σ/S criteria, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank signal, and S is the slope of the linear calibration. The detection limit calculated for sparfloxacin was 0.012 μg/mL. Meanwhile, the ratio of KSV of the MIMSM and NIMSM was defined as the imprinting factor (IF), and it was used to evaluate the specific recognition ability of MIMSM. The IF was calculated to be 2.6 indicating that the fluorescence quenching efficiency and specificity of MIMSM toward sparfloxacin can both be increased during the imprinting process.

To explore the selectivity of MIMSM toward sparfloxacin, structural analogs including norfloxacin, ciprofloxacin, gatifloxacin and clindamycin were selected next. The results and chemical structures of the template molecules and its analogs are shown in Fig. 7 , respectively. Both the MIMSM and NIMSM exhibited a spectroscopic response to the template. As shown in Fig. 7, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of the MIMSM to sparfloxacin is much better than to that of sparfloxacin’s structural analogues. On the other hand, the NIMSM do not exhibit an obvious difference in fluorescence quenching capacity between the template and its structural analogues. The small difference does exist between the fluorescence quenching capacities of sparfloxacin and its structural analogues can be attributed to non-specific binding.

To assess the reproducibility of MIMSM, we fabricated five batches of imprinted materials at different times with the same procedure. The fluorescence response results toward sparfloxacin are shown in Fig. 8 . The imprinted materials exhibited good reproducibility, and the RSD value was 3.2%.

To explain the quenching mechanism of sparfloxacin on the fluorescence of the Mn-ZnS QDs, it is important to understand the fluorescence emission mechanism of the QDs. As shown in Fig. S6, the emission from Mn2+ is excited either by direct recombination of a bound exciton at Mn2+ or via trapping of the hole by Mn2+. Subsequent recombination with shallowly trapped electrons results in Mn2+ in an excited state and is followed by the fluorescence emission [39]. As shown in Fig. S7, in presence of sparfloxacin the fluorescence lifetime of the QDs was decreased, and the quenching mechanism can be described as a photo-induced electron transfer process [47]. The electrons within the QDs are first in an excited state under photo-irradiation. The subsequent electron transfer between the QDs and the sparfloxacin hinders the electronic transition of the d electrons of the Mn2+ to the excited state, which introduces a new nonradioactive decay pathway for the excitation and quenches the fluorescence emission of the Mn-doped ZnS QDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Using MIMSM for selective detection of sparfloxacin in real human serum

ce:section.ce:para: In this work, we demonstrate the preparation and validation of quantum dots embedded in highly ordered molecularly imprinted mesoporous silica microspheres for fluorescence sensing of sparfloxacin in biological samples. Mn-doped ZnS QDs were used as signal elements, and the novel KH-3-MAP was prepared via “thiol-ene” click reaction as a functional monomer; a fluorescent molecularly imprinted sensing material with a highly ordered mesoporous structure was synthesized through a one-step method. Under the optimal conditions, the MIMSM exhibited good specificity and selectivity for the fluorescence recognition of sparfloxacin. In addition, we employed it to analyze sparfloxacin in the real human serum samples and obtained satisfactory results. This indicates that it had great potential in practical application.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21505026, 21275057, and 51478196) and Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangdong Province (2017A020212003 and 2016A020215020).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.10.098.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Molecular imprinting in cross-linked materials with the aid of molecular templates-a way towards artificial antibodies
Selective sample pretreatment by molecularly imprinted polymer monolith for the analysis of fluoroquinolones from milk samples
Multi-template imprinted polymers for simultaneous selective solid-phase extraction of six phenolic compounds in water samples followed by determination using capillary electrophoresis
A novel hydroquinidine imprinted microsphere using a chirality-matching N-acryloyl-l-phenylalanine monomer for recognition of cinchona alkaloids
Hybrid material for protein sensing based on electrosynthesized MIP on a mannose terminated self-assembled monolayer
Molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor based on bioinspired Au microflowers for ultra-trace cholesterol assay
Synthesis of molecularly imprinted polymer on carbon quantum dots as an optical sensor for selective fluorescent determination of promethazine hydrochloride
Development of a selective fluorescence nanosensor based on molecularly imprinted-quantum dot optosensing materials for saxitoxin detection in shellfish samples
Supramolecular imprinted sensor for carbofuran detection based on a functionalized multiwalled carbon nanotube-supported Pd-Ir composite and methylene blue as catalyst
Molecularly imprinted polymers for drug delivery
Active targeting of tumors through conformational epitope imprinting
Too large to fit? Recent developments in macromoleucular imprinting
Graphene-supported ultrafine metal nanoparticles encapsulated by mesoporous silica: robust catalysts for oxidation and reduction reactions
Self-templating synthesis of hollow mesoporous silica and their applications in catalysis and drug delivery
Molecularly imprinted mesoporous silica particles showing a rapid kinetic binding
A molecular imprinting fluorescence sensor based on quantum dots and a mesoporous structure for selective and sensitive detection of 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid
Molecularly imprinted mesoporous silica embedded with carbon dots and semiconductor quantum dots as a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for diniconazole
Controlling morphology and porosity to improve performance of molecularly imprinted sol-gel silica
The rational development of molecularly imprinted polymer-based sensors for protein detection

Semicondutor quantum dots-based metal ion probes
Semiconductor quantum dots in bioanalysis: crossing the valley of death
Carbon dots for optical imaging in vivo
Targeting and imaging cancer cells by Folate-decorated, quantum dots (QDs)-loaded nanoparticles of biodegradable polymers
Visualizing BPA by molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on dual emission nanoparticles
Highly luminescent tetra(biphenyl-4-yl)ethene-grafted molecularly imprinted mesoporous silica nanoparticles for fluorescent sensing of diethylstilbestrol
Molecularly imprinted polymer coated quantum dots for multiplexed cell targeting and imaging
A fluorescent glycosyl-imprinted polymer for pH and temperature regulated sensing of target glycopeptide antibiotic
Molecular imprinting ratiometric fluorescence sensor for highly selective and sensitive detection of phycocyanin
The effect of terfenadine on the cardiac pharmacodynamics of sparfloxacin
Experimental and theoretical studies on the molecular properties of ciprofloxacin, norfloxacin, pefloxacin, sparfloxacin, and gatifloxacin in determining bioavailability
Methods for the analysis of fluoroquinolones in biological fluids
Analytical methods for determination of new fluoroquinolones in biological matrices and pharmaceutical formulations by liquid chromatography: a review
L-Cysteine capped ZnS:Mn quantum dots for room-temperature detection of dopamine with high sensitivity and selectivity
Thiol-ene click reaction as a general route to functional trialkoxysilanes for surface coating applications
Robust, efficient, and orthogonal synthesis of dendrimers viathiol-ene “click” chemistry
Thiol-ene and thiol-yne chemistry in microfluidics: a straightforward method towards macroporous and nonporous functional polymer beads
Selective room temperature phosphorescence sensing of target protein using Mn-doped ZnS QDs-embedded molecularly imprinted polymer
Molecularly imprinted silica nanospheres embedded CdSe quantum dots for highly selective and sensitive optosensing of pyrethroids
Controlling morphology and porosity to improve performance of molecularly imprinted sol-gel silica

Optical sensing of oxygen using a modified Stern–Volmer equation for high laser irradiance
Design of a novel FRET based fluorescent chemosensor and their application for highly sensitive detection of nitroaromatics
Fluorescence probe enhanced spectrofluorimetric method for the determination of sparfloxacin in tablets and biological fluids
Sparfloxacin determination in bulk materials, pharmaceutical formulations and human serum by reverse phase HPLC method
Highly sensitive fluorescence biosensors for sparfloxacin detection at nanogram level based on electron transfer mechanism of cadmium telluride quantum dots
UV-densitometric determination of sparfloxacin and its application to the assay in pharmaceutical dosage forms

Highly sensitive “turn-on” fluorescence probe for the detection of sparfloxacin in human serum using silica-functionalized CdTe quantum dots

Determination of sparfloxacin and its photodegradation products by thin-layer chromatography with densitometry detection. Kinetic evaluation of the degradation process and identification of photoproduct by mass spectrometry
Determination of a series of quinolones in pig plasma using solid-phase extraction and liquid chromatography coupled with mass spectrometric detection: application to pharmacokinetic studies

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yuanyuan Geng is studying for master degree at School of Chemistry and Envriomental, South China Normal University.
Manli Guo is a is an assosicate Professor at School of Chemistry and Envriomental, South China Normal University. Her main research fields are biosensing and environmental analysis.
Jiean Tan is studying for master degree School of Chemistry and Envriomental, South China Normal University.
Shuyi Huang is studying for master degree School of Chemistry and Envriomental, South China Normal University.
Youwen Tang is a Professor at School of Chemistry and Envriomental, South China Normal University. His main research interests are sample preparation, biosensing and environmental analysis.
Lei Tan is a researcher (technician) at Guangzhou Center for Disease Control and Prevention, Guangzhou China. His main research interests are sample preparation, biosensing and HPLC-MS/MS analysis.
Yong Liang is a Professor at School of Chemistry and Envriomental, South China Normal University. His main research interests are sample preparation, biosensing and environmental analysis.
